options.text: It makes the AI train twice as fast.
It allows us to control what kind of image the AI generates.

exercises.explain: A conditional GAN (cGAN) allows us to guide the generator and discriminator by feeding them a label or condition, like asking for a 'cat' instead of a random image.
exercises.prompt: What is the main benefit of adding a 'condition' (like a label) to a GAN?

cards.title: Double-Checking Detective
cards.body: Imagine a security guard who has to check if a ticket is real, but ALSO needs to check if the ticket matches the correct movie! A regular discriminator only checks if the ticket is real. Let's see how a conditional discriminator does double duty!

cards.title: The Two-Step Check
cards.body: In a Conditional GAN, the discriminator evaluates two things: whether the image looks like a real photo, and whether the image matches the provided label (the condition). If either is wrong, the discriminator flags it as fake!

Think of a passport officer. When you show your passport, they don't just check if the passport is a real document. They also check if the face in the photo matches the person standing in front of them. If the passport is real but the face is wrong, they reject it!
cards.title: The Passport Officer
The Mismatched Cat
cards.body: If you give the discriminator a real photo of a cat, but label it 'dog', the discriminator will reject it. It sees that the image is a real photo, but because it doesn't match the 'dog' condition, it marks it as fake.

cards.title: Realism Isn't Everything
cards.body: You might think the discriminator only looks at the image quality. Actually, a perfect photo of a dog will still get rejected as 'fake' by the discriminator if the request was to generate a 'car'. Label matching is just as important as realism!

right.text: A real dog photo paired with the label 'cat' gets rejected as fake.

left.text: A real dog photo paired with the label 'dog' gets approved by the discriminator.

exercises.explain: The discriminator checks both the realism of the image and its match with the label. A real photo of a bird labeled 'car' will be flagged as fake.
exercises.prompt: If we show the discriminator a real photo of a bird but provide the label 'car', what does it do?

options.text: It flags it as fake because the image does not match the label.
It accepts it because the image is a real photo.
It ignores the label and only checks if it is a bird.

exercises.explain: True! To get a 'real' score, the image must look authentic AND correctly match the helper condition label.
exercises.prompt: The discriminator flags an image as fake if the label and the image do not match, even if the image is a real photo.

exercises.explain: If the label (the condition) does not match the image, the discriminator will reject the input, ensuring the generator learns to produce the specific requested object.
exercises.prompt: To pass the discriminator's test, a real photo must match its provided ________.
exercises.sentence: To pass the discriminator's test, a real photo must match its provided [blank].

options.text: The discriminator gets a photo of a dog labeled 'dog' and marks it as real.
The discriminator gets a real photo of a dog labeled 'cat' and marks it as fake.

exercises.explain: In the second case, the discriminator rejects the image because it is given a 'cat' label but shown a 'dog' photo. This enforces conditional generation!
exercises.prompt: Which scenario shows the discriminator enforcing the condition (label)?

options.text: It only checks if the image is real.
It only checks if the label is spelled correctly.
It checks both image realism and if the image matches the label.

exercises.prompt: What are the two requirements for a conditional discriminator to mark an input as 'real'?
exercises.explain: The discriminator requires BOTH criteria to be met: the image must be real AND it must match the label. If either is missing, it returns a fake rating. Now that we understand the discriminator's double-check, let's explore how we train the entire system together!

concept: The discriminator evaluates whether the input is a real photo, and whether it matches the provided label; mismatching real photos are flagged as fake.
title: How the Discriminator Uses the Condition
